reasoning: The recent high yield index hit a peak of 1858.01 as of early December. However, according to the statistical trend analysis provided by the FRED tool, there has been a recent overshoot above the 30-day linear trendline. The statistical baseline points to a near-term mean reversion downward target of around 1855, yielding an initial estimated probability of a short-term increase of ~0.36. Structurally, while high-yield corporate bonds carry a positive forward yield from accrued coupon payments, market commentary heading into 2026 out of Wellington specifically emphasizes that credit spreads are currently extremely tight. This creates asymmetric downside risk of spread widening that could drive near-term price depreciation outstripping the coupon carry. Establishing the FRED tool's mean-reverting statistical probability (0.36 combined estimate) as our explicitly instructed primary anchor, I project the probability of being above the Dec 7th value at ~0.37 over the next week. As we forecast further out, the unyielding mathematical nature of coupon carry (about ~5-6% annually) will begin working to offset any initial price depreciation. Furthermore, in strict accordance with the instruction that forecasting uncertainty must increase over longer horizons (meaning probabilities converge closer to 0.5 over time), the 30-day and 90-day probabilities reflect this structural regression to maximum uncertainty. Thus, the estimates are sequentially adjusted closer to 0.50 for the more distant dates: 0.37 (7 days), 0.42 (30 days), and 0.48 (90 days).
